items.myth: Bayesian analysis must start from a completely objective, data-only viewpoint.
items.reality: Every analysis embeds assumptions; Bayes makes the prior explicit. Even 'uninformative' priors encode choices about parameterization and scale.
items.example: Uniform on θ ∈ (0,1) for a coin differs from uniform on logit(θ)—posteriors change under reparameterization.

items.myth: More data always wash out any prior.
items.reality: Likelihood eventually dominates only when the prior is proper and not infinitely sharp; with moderate n, informative priors still pull MAP/posterior means.
items.example: After 10 flips, Beta(100,100) prior still keeps posterior mean closer to 0.5 than Beta(1,1) does for the same data.

items.myth: The posterior is a single number—you either believe θ or you don't.
items.reality: The posterior is a full distribution—mean, mode, variance and credible intervals all matter, especially for skewed posteriors.
items.example: Beta(3,97) and Beta(30,970) share mean 0.03 but differ in uncertainty; reporting only the mean hides risk.
